8	---
9	# Feature: Task Execution
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx owns the task execution lifecycle. The lifecycle is made of concrete layers
19	with stable public names: `run`, `try`, and `work`. Each higher layer composes
20	the layer beneath it. There are no other run kinds beyond those named layers.
21	
22	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
23	|---|---|---|---|---|
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + execution config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
27	
28	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
29	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
30	
31	Implementation code for this boundary should follow the task-execution
32	vocabulary (`taskexec` for core layer logic, `fizeauadapter` for the service
33	bridge) rather than the legacy `internal/agent` package name. Those package
34	names should make the DDx-owned workflow boundary explicit.
35	
36	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 when the
37	generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata. It is
38	not a fourth layer — it is a labeled invocation at an existing layer.
39	
40	## Agent Interaction Modes
41	
42	DDx defines four named agent interaction modes (`DDX_MODE`) to separate
43	broad interactive queue stewardship from worker execution. The mode governs
44	what an agent opened in a DDx project is permitted to do.
45	
46	| DDX_MODE | Description | Allowed actions | Prohibited actions |
47	|---|---|---|---|
48	| `queue_steward` | Default interactive mode. Surveys, triages, and advises on the queue without claiming or executing beads. | Read tracker and docs; report status; advise on bead quality; suggest readiness fixes; run `ddx bead list/ready/status/show`. | Claiming beads; creating isolated worktrees; starting `ddx work` or `ddx try` without an explicit user directive. |
49	| `bead_execution` | Worker mode. Executes one or more beads from the queue in isolated worktrees. | Full FEAT-010 layer-2 / layer-3 lifecycle: worktree creation, bead attempt, merge or preserve, evidence bundle. | Scope creep outside the named bead; switching to direct implementation without a bead. |
50	| `direct_user_implementation` | Human-directed implementation. The user has explicitly asked the agent to edit code or docs, bypassing the bead queue. | Edit code and docs as instructed; commit to the current branch. | Starting autonomous queue drain; claiming beads autonomously. |
51	| `review` | Read-only review. Grades an existing implementation against AC or specs. | Read code, docs, and evidence; return a structured verdict with per-AC findings. | Writing commits; claiming beads; modifying the tracker. |
52	
53	### Precedence
54	
55	`bead_execution` supersedes `queue_steward` when DDx invokes a worker
56	explicitly (via `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, or `ddx agent execute-bead`).
57	`direct_user_implementation` supersedes `queue_steward` when the user
58	explicitly asks for code or doc edits unrelated to the queue. `review`
59	supersedes `queue_steward` when the user explicitly requests a graded review
60	pass.
61	
62	Tracker instructions, merge-policy, and safety rules in AGENTS.md are
63	load-bearing in every mode and are never overridden by mode selection.
64	
65	### Worker-capacity suggestion rules
66	
67	An agent in `queue_steward` mode may suggest running `ddx work` or point
68	the operator to worker-capacity settings when:
69	
70	- The queue contains execution-ready beads that have not been attempted.
71	- The queue is blocked and operator action is required.
72	- The operator asks "why isn't anything running?" or a similar capacity question.
73	
74	The agent must not autonomously start `ddx work` from `queue_steward` mode.
75	Suggesting the command or explaining the conditions is the correct action.
76	
77	## Problem Statement
78	
79	**Current situation:** DDx has accreted two independent run-storage shapes
80	— `.ddx/exec-runs/` for generic execution evidence and
81	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` for tracked bead-attempt bundles. Loop behavior,
82	worktree lifecycle, and bead resolution are spread across legacy command
83	surfaces without a clean layering story. There is no single place to introspect "what runs
84	happened, at what layer, with what evidence."
85	
86	**Pain points:**
87	- Two on-disk record shapes for fundamentally similar artifacts (a single
88	  agent invocation, with logs, structured result, and provenance)
89	- No first-class concept of the task execution layers (`run` / `try` /
90	  `work`), so consumers must intuit which surface to read
91	- Stop conditions for queue drain are implicit and not observable
92	- Generated-artifact provenance has no clean hook into the run record
93	- Open-ended catalog pressure: every new "run flavor" (benchmark, replay,
94	  comparison) tempts a new on-disk shape or a new top-level verb
95	
96	**Desired outcome:** A single on-disk run substrate with explicit layer
97	metadata, three top-level CLI verbs that map 1:1 onto the layers, and a
98	narrow read-only HTTP/MCP write surface limited to artifact regeneration.
99	
100	## Task Execution Layers
101	
102	### Layer 1 — `ddx run` (invocation atom)
103	
104	A layer-1 run is one AI invocation. Inputs are a prompt, requested
105	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`, optional operator passthrough constraints,
106	and non-routing execution config; outputs are the structured response (text or
107	bytes), any side-effects the service performed via tools, and run metadata
108	(tokens, model, actual power, duration, exit status, session pointer).
109	
110	Layer 1 calls the upstream Fizeau execution contract directly. DDx does not
111	reimplement routing or the invocation loop; it wraps one `Execute` call with
112	provenance capture.
113	
114	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is layer 1 when the generator returns
115	bytes/text and DDx writes the file; it is layer 2 when the generator
116	edits the repo directly (bead-less worktree).
117	
118	### Layer 2 — `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt)
119	
120	A layer-2 run is one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. It owns:
121	
122	- Worktree creation from a base revision and worktree finalization
123	  (merge or preserve)
124	- Worktree cleanup after finalization, failed setup, failed publish, and
125	  ordinary interruption
126	- Bead → prompt resolution (description, acceptance, governing artifacts)
127	- Side-effect bundling (commits, evidence, no-changes rationale)
128	- One or more layer-1 invocations recorded inside the attempt record
129	
130	A layer-2 record references its child layer-1 records by run id.
131	
132	Layer 2 owns bead-attempt success classification. DDx determines success from
133	the artifacts it owns: commit presence, merge/preserve result, no-changes
134	rationale, post-run checks, review verdicts, and cooldown policy. The agent's
135	exit status and actual model/power are inputs to that decision, not the whole
136	decision.
137	
138	Layer 2 records raw attempt evidence. Layer 3 applies
139	[`TD-031`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md) when
140	that evidence requires durable bead action such as close-as-already-satisfied,
141	human triage, blocked, retry cooldown, or stale no_changes metadata cleanup.
142	
143	### Quality hooks
144	
145	ADR-023 defines two lifecycle quality hooks owned by the layer-2 and layer-3
146	execution loop. They are hooks inside `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, not new run
147	layers and not new bead-schema fields.
148	
149	`BeadReadinessHook` is the product concept: it runs after the worker has
150	selected a dependency-ready candidate but before DDx claims the bead or
151	creates the implementation worktree. The canonical decision being made is bead
152	readiness assessment, the pre-claim check for tractability and actionability;
153	lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
154	triage is a separate after-evidence action.
155	The implementation may still call the compatibility entrypoint
156	`PreClaimIntakeHook` and record `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility, but
157	those names are aliases only. Within readiness, the nested bead-lifecycle
158	workflow skill performs lint/rubric scoring to support the decision, and the
159	score is diagnostic evidence rather than a separate queue action. Post-attempt
160	triage is separate and runs only after evidence exists. The hook has enough
161	context to evaluate title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, parent,
162	deps, bead type, spec-id, prior attempt history, and whether the bead is
163	atomic enough to execute. The hook invokes the nested bead-lifecycle workflow
164	skill under the `ddx` skill tree and records readiness evidence in the
165	layer-3 run record.
166	
167	`ddx work` wires this hook by default in both CLI and server-managed worker
168	paths. Decomposition decisions run with a strong `MinPower` floor, defaulting to
169	the smart/top-power tier floor when no project-specific splitter override is
170	configured. DDx does not choose the concrete model; it passes the raised power
171	floor to Fizeau and preserves any operator-supplied harness/provider/model
172	passthrough constraints. If those constraints cannot satisfy the strong floor,
173	DDx records `agent_power_unsatisfied` and blocks instead of running weak
174	decomposition.
175	
176	The readiness assessment result is one of:
177	
178	- `actionable_atomic` — claim and execute normally.
179	- `actionable_but_rewritten` — DDx applied a validated replacement rewrite or
180	  metadata update that makes the bead more execution-ready before claim. Safe
181	  rewrites may replace noisy, stale, duplicated, or brittle description text
182	  with a fit-for-purpose bead body instead of appending more prompt burden. They may
183	  also format the description into the authoring template, add discovered
184	  file:line evidence, add an obvious test command, or wire deterministic
185	  labels/parent/deps. The mutation is recorded through `ddx bead` paths before
186	  claim, with before/after hashes and enough evidence to audit the replacement.
187	- `too_large_decomposed` — DDx created child beads, mapped every parent AC to
188	  child ACs or an explicit `needs_human` / `non_scope` marker, blocked the
189	  parent, and did not execute the parent.
190	- `ambiguous_needs_human` — the bead/spec is unclear, contradictory,
191	  unverifiable, or missing acceptance criteria that DDx cannot safely invent.
192	  DDx blocks or marks the bead `needs_human` and does not claim it.
193	- `readiness_error` — readiness infrastructure failed. In migration/WARN mode
194	  this fails open with evidence; in reliable factory/BLOCK mode it may skip the
195	  candidate for the current pass but must not park it behind a cooldown unless a
196	  retryable time-based class is recorded.
197	
198	Safe rewrites are validated against durable context, not against a vague
199	"same spirit" comparison to the prior bead text. The prior bead can itself be
200	the problem: it may contain stale line numbers, duplicated history, chat-shaped
201	noise, or contradictory wording. A safe replacement must preserve explicit
202	commitments that remain valid, especially acceptance criteria, non-scope, named
203	files/tests, dependencies, and governing artifact references; it must either
204	carry forward durable root-cause evidence or replace it with current file:line
205	evidence from the repository or governing artifact. The original text is kept in
206	readiness evidence, not copied into the replacement body. Rewrites must optimize
207	prompt fitness rather than raw length: expanding a one-line underspecified bead
208	is safe when the added durable context is required for execution, and compressing
209	a noisy bead is safe when explicit commitments remain preserved.
210	
211	Readiness must reject the rewrite and park the bead as `ambiguous_needs_human`
212	when preservation cannot be proven from durable anchors. It must not invent
213	product behavior, choose between conflicting requirements, change scope, delete
214	unresolved constraints, or guess a missing governing artifact.
215	
216	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its local evidence:
217	agent result, commit/no-commit state, no-changes rationale if any, post-run
218	checks, adversarial review verdict, merge/preserve result, and the readiness
219	report.
220	It uses the same bead-lifecycle skill to classify whether the outcome is a
221	normal attempt result, a quality-policy failure, a missing-evidence failure, or
222	an infrastructure failure. This is distinct from bead readiness assessment and
223	from the lint/rubric scoring used inside readiness. The hook must never rewrite
224	the attempt result or erase artifacts; it only adds triage evidence and feeds
225	retry/stop classification.
226	
227	If post-attempt triage finds that an implementation attempt stopped because the
228	bead was too large or the worker could not legally decompose inside its
229	worktree/depth context, the layer-3 worker must invoke the same orchestrator
230	decomposition path used by `BeadReadinessHook`. This is machine-actionable
231	work: DDx files child beads, records the AC map, and blocks the parent unless
232	the split is lossy, ambiguous, or at the queue-level decomposition depth cap.
233	The operator is not required merely because the implementer could not split
234	from inside the attempted execution.
235	
236	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains `OutcomeReason` beside the existing `Disrupted`
237	signal. `Disrupted` remains the mechanical indicator that normal completion was
238	interrupted. `OutcomeReason` is the stable classification string that explains
239	why the attempt ended, such as `lint_warn`, `lint_blocked`,
240	`lint_hook_error_fail_open`, `readiness_ambiguous`, `readiness_decomposed`,
241	`triage_missing_evidence`, `review_fixable_gap`, `review_spec_gap`,
242	`review_too_large`, `post_run_check_failed`, or `success`. Layer-3 `ddx work`
243	records aggregate these reasons when evaluating retry eligibility, no-progress,
244	blocked, and deferred stop conditions.
245	
246	The hook evidence lives in the attempt evidence bundle and is surfaced through
247	the unified run substrate. The originating bead may receive an evidence event
248	only for explicit operator override (`--force --reason`) or other existing
249	audit events; lint scores are not copied into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
250	
251	### Layer 3 — `ddx work` (queue drain)
252	
253	A layer-3 run is one drain of the bead queue. It iterates `ddx try`
254	across ready beads until a stop condition is met. It owns:
255	
256	- Queue iteration order
257	- Pre-claim readiness, safe bead improvement, decomposition, claim acquisition,
258	  claim release, and shutdown/interruption cleanup for claimed beads, using the
259	  TD-031 claim-state contract
260	- Durable bead action after each layer-2 attempt, using TD-031's outcome,
261	  no_changes, cooldown, and stale-metadata rules
262	- No-progress / stop-condition evaluation
263	- A loop-level record that references its child layer-2 records by
264	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,
265	  deferred, no-progress)
266	
267	Content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue
268	reconciliation beads") are not layer-3 concerns. Those are skill or
269	plugin compositions on top of the task execution layers.
270	
271	Layer 3 treats raw attempt evidence separately from durable bead action. When a
272	worker stops, receives SIGTERM/SIGINT, loses its child agent process, or honors
273	operator cancel before a terminal bead mutation, it follows TD-031: preserve
274	evidence, record the interruption/disruption, release the claim, and leave the
275	bead re-claimable unless an explicit retryable cooldown or blocker was recorded.
276	Shutdown is not itself a reason to park a bead on `execute-loop-retry-after`.
277	
278	## Agent Power and Retry
279	
280	DDx owns retry policy between bead attempts. If an attempt produces classified
281	evidence that more capable reasoning could plausibly change the result, DDx may
282	retry the bead with a higher `MinPower`.
283	
284	Fizeau owns routing within the requested power bounds. A retry request says
285	"use at least this much power" by raising `MinPower`; an explicit `MaxPower`, if
286	supplied, remains a hard upper bound. Operator-supplied `--harness`,
287	`--provider`, and `--model` values may be sent to Fizeau as passthrough
288	constraints, but DDx does not interpret them. Each layer-1 record stores:
289	
290	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
291	- requested passthrough constraints, if any
292	- actual model and actual power reported by Fizeau
293	- run outcome and DDx attempt outcome
294	
295	DDx may query Fizeau's available model/power catalog to choose escalation
296	targets such as "retry using only top-power models." DDx uses the returned power
297	numbers only to compute `MinPower` thresholds; Fizeau still chooses the
298	concrete model.
299	
300	Passthrough constraints stay sticky across retries. DDx may increase `MinPower`
301	between attempts, but it must not alter or drop operator-supplied
302	harness/provider/model passthrough values to make escalation work. If the
303	combination is invalid or constraining, the agent owns that typed error or
304	actual route. DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
305	`agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the evidence, and reports operator action
306	required; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`, or retry in a
307	loop.
308	
309	### Retry eligibility
310	
311	Power retry is eligible only when DDx-owned evidence shows that a stronger model
312	could plausibly help after a valid attempt started. Eligible classes include:
313	
314	- `capability_insufficient` — the agent attempted the bead, but reasoning
315	  quality or implementation quality was insufficient.
316	- `post_run_check_failed` — tests or gates failed after a valid checkout and
317	  attempted change.
318	- `review_fixable_gap` — adversarial review found implementation or test gaps
319	  plausibly addressable by another automated cycle.
320	- `no_changes_after_attempt` — the agent had a valid checkout and task context
321	  but produced no usable change, subject to the no-progress budget.
322	
323	Power retry is not eligible for deterministic setup or operator-action
324	failures:
325	
326	- dirty worktree, merge/land conflict, missing checkout, invalid bead metadata,
327	  unresolved dependencies, config parse errors, missing harness binaries,
328	  authentication failures, command-not-found/toolchain setup failures
329	- readiness ambiguity, missing acceptance criteria, spec contradictions,
330	  decomposition overflow, review errors, reviewer context overflow, claim races,
331	  routing preflight rejection, quota/transport disruption, and any other class
332	  where the implementer did not receive valid task context and an opportunity to
333	  act
334	- `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` / `agent_power_unsatisfied`
335	- any status where retry would require DDx to inspect, remove, rewrite, or
336	  substitute `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`
337	
338	Every retry/stop decision records the classification and evidence used. The
339	policy may inspect DDx attempt outcomes and the agent's typed status; it must
340	not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
341	
342	ADR-024 is the decision record for this policy. FEAT-010 owns when `ddx try` or
343	`ddx work` schedules a retry, how no-progress and stop conditions are recorded,
344	how review-error retry scopes are counted, and how budget caps stop a drain.
345	FEAT-006 owns only the agent request envelope used by those decisions.
346	
347	### Execution Cycle Traceability
348	
349	A bead execution is an append-only chain of implementation/review cycles. Layer
350	3 owns the chain; layer 2 owns the evidence for each cycle. A repair attempt
351	must create a new cycle record rather than overwrite the rejected one.
352	
353	Each cycle records:
354	
355	- `cycle_index`
356	- `attempt_id`
357	- `base_rev`
358	- `result_rev` when a stable candidate exists
359	- implementer layer-1 run ids, requested power bounds, actual route/power, and
360	  cost/usage facts
361	- verification command output and exit status
362	- `review_group_id`, reviewer layer-1 run ids, reviewer requested/actual
363	  route facts, aggregate verdict, per-AC evidence summary, and reviewer cost
364	- prior review linkage (`repair_context_from_review_group`) when this cycle is
365	  a repair attempt
366	- final cycle decision: close, retry, decompose, block, manual-required, or
367	  budget stop
368	
369	When a review requires another implementation cycle, the next implementation
370	prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required repair context.
371	The prior cycle remains immutable so operators can answer what changed, why the
372	first result was rejected, and why the final result passed.
373	
374	### Candidate-Cycle Pipeline
375	
376	A **candidate** is the stable commit produced by one implementation pass and
377	pinned as the subject of the review gate before land. A candidate exists when
378	the implementation produces at least one commit and post-run checks have been
379	attempted.
380	
381	A **candidate ref** is a project-root git ref
382	(`refs/ddx/iterations/<attempt-id>/<cycle-index>`) that pins the candidate
383	commit so reviewers can inspect a stable revision even after worktree cleanup.
384	
385	A **cycle** is one implementation pass plus its associated review gate: one
386	implementation pass that produces a candidate, the read-only tool reviewer gate
387	run against that candidate in the still-live worktree, and the resulting
388	disposition (land, repair, or stop).
389	
390	A **repair cycle** is a cycle triggered when the prior cycle's review verdict is
391	`review_fixable_gap`. The repair implementation runs in the same still-live
392	attempt worktree and is **append-only** — no `git reset`, `git commit --amend`,
393	`git squash`, or `git rebase` against the prior candidate is permitted. The
394	repair prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required context.
395	
396	A **read-only tool reviewer** is a reviewer agent that runs in the still-live
397	attempt worktree with access to read-only filesystem tools (file reads,
398	searches) but no write tools. The bounded evidence bundle assembled per FEAT-022
399	is the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access is
400	supplemental and does not override the bounded bundle. The reviewer cannot
401	create commits or modify worktree state.
402	
403	**Terminal dispositions** for a candidate-cycle attempt:
404	- `landed` — candidate was approved and merged to the base branch.
405	- `preserved` — candidate is approved but land was deferred; worktree retained.
406	- `parked` — repair budget exhausted without approval; bead moved to `needs_human`.
407	- `conflicted` — approved candidate failed to land due to merge conflict; bead
408	  returned to `open` for re-attempt with a fresh `base_rev`.
409	- `budget-stopped` — drain-level cost or no-progress budget tripped; worktree
410	  preserved with evidence.
411	
412	**Candidate-cycle sequence** (in the still-live attempt worktree, before land):
413	
414	1. Create isolated worktree from `base_rev`.
415	2. Run implementation pass; collect `base_rev`, `result_rev`, run ids,
416	   verification output, and cost facts.
417	3. Pin candidate ref; emit `candidate-pinned`.
418	4. Run candidate checks (post-run tests, lint, gates). Failure emits
419	   `candidate-checks-failed`; plausibly capability-sensitive failures are
420	   eligible for a repair cycle when `repair_max_cycles` allows.
421	5. Dispatch read-only tool reviewers in the still-live worktree, **before land**.
422	   Record `review_group_id`, reviewer run ids, per-AC verdict, and cost.
423	6. On unanimous `APPROVE`: land, emit `final-result-landed`, close bead, clean up.
424	7. On `REQUEST_CHANGES` / `BLOCK` classified `review_fixable_gap` within
425	   `repair_max_cycles`: emit `repair-cycle-started`, run append-only repair in
426	   same worktree, return to step 3.
427	8. On `repair_max_cycles` exhausted: emit `repair-cycle-exhausted`, preserve
428	   worktree, park bead in `needs_human`.
429	9. On approved candidate that fails to land (merge conflict): emit
430	   `approved-land-conflict`, release claim, return bead to `open`.
431	
432	**Budget axes** (each is distinct from cost, rate-limit/provider, and
433	no-progress budgets):
434	
435	- **`repair_max_cycles`** — maximum repair cycles per attempt. Exhaustion fires
436	  `repair-cycle-exhausted` and parks the bead in `needs_human`; it does not
437	  consume the no-progress counter.
438	- **`review_max_retries_per_candidate`** — maximum reviewer retry attempts for a
439	  single candidate ref when reviewer invocations fail. Exhaustion fires
440	  `review-manual-required`. A new candidate ref (next repair cycle or fresh
441	  `ddx try`) resets this counter independently; it is strictly per-candidate.
442	
443	These axes do not interact with the drain-level cost cap (FEAT-014), the
444	rate-limit retry budget internal to a single attempt, or the no-progress counter
445	tracking consecutive attempts with no commit.
446	
447	**Transitional single-slot allowance.** Until the two-slot quorum reviewer bead
448	lands, a single reviewer slot satisfies the review gate. The removal condition
449	is the landing of the bead that implements two-slot quorum aggregation.
450	
451	### Escalation, fallback, retry, and review decision tree
452	
453	The layer-3 drain evaluates each ready bead through this mechanical sequence:
454	
455	1. **Eligibility and readiness.** Pick a dependency-ready candidate. Run the
456	   readiness gate. Validated replacement rewrites happen before claim and should
457	   leave a clearer implementation prompt, expanding underspecified beads or
458	   compressing noisy/stale beads as the durable context requires; original text
459	   is preserved in readiness evidence for audit. Too-large work is decomposed
460	   before an implementation attempt. Ambiguous or underspecified work is blocked
461	   with `needs_human`. Readiness infrastructure failure records evidence and
462	   follows the configured fail-open/factory-mode policy; it never creates an
463	   unexplained cooldown.
464	2. **Claim.** Claim only an `actionable_atomic` or safely rewritten bead. Claim
465	   races skip the bead for the current pass without cooldown.
466	3. **Primary implementation cycle.** Run one layer-2 implementation attempt.
467	   A candidate result records `base_rev`, `result_rev`, implementation run ids,
468	   route/power facts, verification output, and cost. Already-satisfied
469	   no-changes may close only after the required verification evidence exists;
470	   otherwise no-changes outcomes follow TD-031.
471	4. **Adversarial pre-close review.** Review is enabled by default. For every
472	   close-eligible `result_rev`, layer 2 pins a candidate ref and dispatches two
473	   read-only tool reviewer runs **in the still-live attempt worktree, before
474	   land**, each carrying `role=reviewer`, a stronger `MinPower` floor than the
475	   implementer, and correlation metadata (`review_group_id`, `result_rev`,
476	   reviewer slot, and implementer route facts). The bounded evidence bundle is
477	   the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access is
478	   supplemental. Close is permitted only when the aggregate review is unanimous
479	   `APPROVE` with per-AC evidence.
480	5. **Review classification.** Any evidenced `REQUEST_CHANGES` or `BLOCK`
481	   prevents close. `review_fixable_gap` schedules a repair cycle on the same
482	   bead when `repair_max_cycles` allows, injecting the review findings as
483	   required repair context and optionally raising `MinPower`; it preserves the
484	   retry path and does not park the bead in the `needs_human` lane.
485	   `review_spec_gap`, `review_missing_acceptance`, `review_too_large`, and
486	   non-mechanical unsafe or out-of-scope findings park the bead in the
487	   `needs_human` operator-attention lane, clear the active claim, and do not ask
488	   another implementer to guess. Malformed, empty, context-overflow, and
489	   transport reviewer failures emit `review-error` scoped to `result_rev` and
490	   reviewer slot; after `review_max_retries_per_candidate` they emit
491	   `review-manual-required`, clear the active claim, park the bead in the
492	   `needs_human` lane, and do not close. The `review_fixable_gap` path stays on
493	   the automatic retry track instead of converting into operator review.
494	6. **Infrastructure fallback.** Transport, quota, rate-limit, command setup,
495	   context cancellation, routing preflight rejection, and worker disruption are
496	   not model-capability failures. They emit structured evidence and either stay
497	   immediately retryable or use a bounded retry-after when the same class is
498	   time-based. HTTP 429 retry happens inside one attempt with `rate-limit-retry`
499	   events.
500	7. **Capability retry.** Failed checks, `review_fixable_gap`,
501	   capability-insufficient attempts, and eligible no-changes-after-attempt may
502	   schedule a higher-power retry by raising the next request's `MinPower`.
503	   Passthrough constraints remain unchanged. If the requested power cannot be
504	   satisfied under those constraints, DDx records a terminal operator-action
505	   classification and stops retrying that bead.
506	8. **Cost stop.** Implementation and review attempts both contribute reported
507	   billable cost to the drain budget according to FEAT-014 cost-class metadata.
508	   When the cap trips, the drain records a budget stop and stops claiming new
509	   work; the cap is not reported as model failure.
510	
511	## Substrate Unification
512	
513	### One record shape
514	
515	All task execution layers persist to a single on-disk shape under
516	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. The record has the following common fields and a
517	discriminating `layer` field; layer-specific extensions live in named
518	subobjects rather than separate file trees.
519	
520	Common fields:
521	- `run_id` — stable, sortable identifier
522	- `layer` — `1` | `2` | `3`
523	- `parent_run_id` — pointer to the enclosing layer's run, if any
524	- `started_at` / `finished_at`
525	- `terminal_status` — `success` | `failure` | `timeout` | `error` |
526	  `no_progress` (layer 3 only) | `preserved` (layer 2 only)
527	- `actor`, `host`, `git_revision`, `ddx_version`
528	- `produces_artifact` — optional artifact id when this run regenerates
529	  an artifact
530	- `attachments[]` — references to large bodies (logs, structured payloads,
531	  bundles) stored alongside the record rather than inline
532	
533	Layer extensions:
534	- `layer1` — prompt reference, agent config, model id, token usage,
535	  upstream session id, structured response pointer
536	- `layer2` — bead id, base revision, worktree path, finalization mode
537	  (`merge` | `preserve`), child layer-1 run ids, evidence-bundle pointer
538	- `layer3` — queue snapshot pointer, stop-condition evaluation log,
539	  child layer-2 attempt ids, drain disposition
540	
541	Records are append-only. A new run never rewrites a previous one.
542	
543	### On-disk migration
544	
545	The two pre-existing trees collapse into the unified substrate:
546	
547	- `.ddx/exec-runs/<run-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1`
548	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 2`
549	  and the existing prompt / manifest / result / checks / provenance
550	  surfaced as layer-2 evidence-bundle attachments
551	
552	Layer-3 records are new (no pre-existing tree).
553	
554	Migration is one-way and read-compatible:
555	- Writers always target `.ddx/runs/`
556	- Readers tolerate legacy paths during a documented migration window
557	  and surface them through the unified read API as if they were layer-1
558	  or layer-2 records, then a one-shot rewrite normalizes them on disk
559	- After the migration window closes, legacy paths are removed; readers
560	  no longer fall back
561	
562	The migration window, the rewrite tool, and the cutoff signal are
563	specified in the implementation epic, not here.
564	
565	## `ddx work` Stop Conditions
566	
567	`ddx work` drains the queue until **any** of the following stop
568	conditions is met. The terminal disposition of the layer-3 record names
569	which condition fired.
570	
571	1. **`drained`** — queue is empty (no ready beads remain).
572	2. **`blocked`** — every remaining ready bead has produced a terminal
573	   non-success outcome on its most recent attempt and re-attempt is not
574	   permitted under the current policy.
575	3. **`deferred`** — a configured wall-clock or attempt-count budget is
576	   exhausted. The remaining queue is intact and a subsequent `ddx work`
577	   resumes from where this one stopped.
578	4. **`no_progress`** — `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produced
579	   no commit and no merged side-effect on the base branch (default `N = 3`;
580	   configurable). A valid implementation attempt is one where DDx created a
581	   checkout, supplied task context to the implementer, and the implementer had
582	   an opportunity to act. Intake blocks, decomposition, claim races, routing
583	   preflight rejection, quota/transport/auth/tool setup failures, review errors,
584	   and operator-action classes do not increment this counter.
585	5. **`signal`** — a SIGINT/SIGTERM was received. On the first signal, DDx
586	   prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully`, cancels the active
587	   command context, stops claiming new beads, and gives the in-flight
588	   `ddx try` (if any) one cooperative cleanup path to preserve evidence,
589	   release its claim, and leave the bead re-claimable. A second signal may
590	   hard-abort the process.
591	6. **`resource_exhausted`** — DDx cannot safely create or publish execution
592	   state because a required execution root is out of bytes, out of inodes, not
593	   writable, or cannot register/remove git worktrees. Resource exhaustion is
594	   host infrastructure failure, not bead failure and not model no-progress.
595	   `ddx work` stops claiming new beads after recording the failed `ddx try`
596	   setup evidence and a cleanup summary. It must not continue to the next
597	   ready bead in the same drain.
598	
599	Stop-condition evaluation normally runs **between** `ddx try` invocations.
600	`signal` is the exception: the first signal cancels an in-flight attempt
601	cooperatively rather than waiting for the model to finish. The cancelled
602	attempt is mechanically disrupted, not a model no-progress failure, and ordinary
603	interrupt shutdown MUST NOT park the bead behind `execute-loop-retry-after`.
604	
605	### Execution Resource Cleanup
606	
607	DDx-created execution resources must have an owner, a liveness signal, a
608	retention policy, and a cleanup path. Missing ownership, missing liveness, or
609	unbounded accumulation is a correctness bug in the execution substrate.
610	
611	Layer 2 owns inline cleanup for one `ddx try` attempt. Before claim or worktree
612	creation it validates the required execution roots:
613	
614	- the temporary worktree root, currently `$TMPDIR/ddx-exec-wt` unless
615	  overridden
616	- the durable evidence root, currently `.ddx/executions` during migration and
617	  `.ddx/runs` for new substrate records
618	- git worktree registration/removal for the project repository
619	
620	The validation checks writability, free bytes, and free inodes where the
621	platform exposes inode counts. If validation fails, `ddx try` runs one
622	immediate DDx-scoped cleanup pass, re-checks, and then either proceeds or
623	returns `resource_exhausted` without claiming the bead.
624	
625	`ddx work` and server-managed workers use the same cleanup manager before the
626	first claim and before later claims whenever temp free space drops below a soft
627	high-water threshold. If cleanup does not restore the temp roots above the hard
628	floor, the loop stops visibly with `resource_exhausted` and claims no more
629	beads.
630	
631	After an attempt starts, Layer 2 removes the isolated worktree when the result
632	has been merged, explicitly preserved, classified as no-changes/no-evidence, or
633	interrupted through the cooperative shutdown path. Failed setup must remove any
634	partial unregistered directory it created. A worktree may remain only when DDx
635	records an explicit preserve decision with evidence pointing at the retained
636	path or ref.
637	
638	DDx-owned cleanup scope includes execution worktrees, DDx-created test and e2e
639	scratch roots, generated test binaries, and run-state or liveness files. The
640	cleanup manager may delete only DDx-owned paths with ownership metadata or
641	known DDx prefixes, minimum age/mtime satisfied, and no live PID/session
642	liveness. It must preserve published evidence, registered active worktrees,
643	and anything outside DDx-owned roots.
644	
645	Layer 3 owns loop cleanup. `ddx work` runs cleanup:
646	
647	- once at startup, before the first queue claim
648	- before the next claim after any setup/finalization failure
649	- periodically while a long-lived poll worker remains active
650	- during graceful signal shutdown before exit
651	
652	Long-lived DDx processes also start a background cleanup worker. The background
653	worker runs occasionally with jitter and a project-level cleanup lock so multiple
654	workers do not all prune at once. It is conservative and DDx-scoped: it may
655	remove stale unregistered directories under DDx temp roots, registered DDx
656	worktrees whose attempt is terminal or whose liveness marker is stale, stale
657	heartbeat/liveness files for dead PIDs, and partial setup directories that were
658	never published as complete evidence. It must not remove preserved worktrees,
659	`refs/ddx/iterations/...`, complete `.ddx/runs/<id>` or
660	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` evidence, active worktrees with live
661	PID/session liveness, or non-DDx directories.
662	
663	Cleanup is observable but not noisy. Routine passes are trace/debug or worker
664	events. Passes that reclaim significant bytes or inodes emit an operator-visible
665	summary such as `cleanup: removed 37 stale ddx worktrees, freed 14210 inodes`.
666	Resource exhaustion after cleanup is a hard visible stop message and a layer-3
667	`resource_exhausted` disposition.
668	
669	Cleanup reporting includes scratch roots removed, bytes and inodes reclaimed,
670	preserved paths, and blocked warnings so operators can see why cleanup stopped
671	short.
672	
673	### Long-running default (`--poll-interval`)
674	
675	Per ddx-dc157075, the default `--poll-interval` for `ddx work` is **30s**.
676	With a positive poll interval the
677	worker stays alive across empty polls (`drained` becomes a transient
678	"running (idle)" substate, not a terminal exit). The loop exits only on
679	the conditions enumerated above plus `signal`, fatal config errors, or
680	the explicit operator opt-outs:
681	
682	- `--once` — process at most one ready bead, then exit.
683	- `--poll-interval=0` — legacy "drain-and-exit" semantics: when the
684	  queue empties, return immediately without polling.
685	
686	Server-managed workers spawned via `POST /api/workers/execute-loop`
687	inherit the same 30s default when the request omits `poll_interval`. The
688	worker record exposes a `substate` field set to `"idle"` while the loop
689	is sleeping between empty polls; it is cleared as soon as a candidate is
690	picked. Terminal worker states (`exited`, `failed`, `stopped`, `reaped`)
691	always clear `substate`.
692	
693	The structured `loop.end` event carries an `exit_reason` field
694	(`once_complete`, `explicit_poll_zero`, `sigint`, `sigterm`,
695	`fatal_config`, `resource_exhausted`; `providers_exhausted` is reserved for the
696	quota-pause work in ddx-aede917d).
697	
698	Power escalation is evaluated between attempts as part of retry policy. A
699	higher-power retry raises `MinPower` and resets neither the evidence history nor
700	the no-progress counter; it is recorded as the next `ddx try` with its own
701	requested bounds and actual power metadata.
702	
703	The evaluation log is persisted on the layer-3 record so a human or
704	tool can audit which condition fired and on which iteration.
705	
706	## Quality Hooks
707	
708	ADR-023 (`../../02-design/adr/ADR-023-bead-lifecycle-quality-policy.md`)
709	adds two quality hooks to the layer-2/layer-3 lifecycle. The hooks are
710	implemented in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime` at the same boundary that already
711	owns bead selection, attempt finalization, and retry classification.
712	
713	`BeadReadinessHook` runs after a bead has been selected and verified as
714	dependency-eligible, but before DDx claims it or creates the implementation
715	worktree. It performs the canonical bead readiness assessment, the pre-claim
716	decision about tractability and actionability; lint/rubric scoring is the
717	diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt triage is a separate
718	after-evidence action. The legacy compatibility name `PreClaimIntakeHook` and
719	`MODE: intake` may still appear in migration code or notes, but they are not
720	the product concept.
721	
722	The hook receives the bead record, current execution policy, hook mode
723	(`WARN-ONLY` or `BLOCK`), and the layer-3 evidence directory. It invokes the
724	bead-lifecycle workflow skill from FEAT-011 and writes a bead readiness,
725	scope, and decomposition report. In WARN-ONLY mode, the report is diagnostic
726	unless the hook can safely improve or decompose the bead. In BLOCK/factory
727	mode, a valid low readiness score, unsafe ambiguity, or too-large
728	classification stops implementation before claim.
729	
730	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its owned
731	evidence: commits or no-changes rationale, command results, review verdicts,
732	merge/preserve result, and any structural validation result. It invokes the
733	same bead-lifecycle workflow skill against the attempt bundle and returns an
734	attempt classification such as prompt-quality issue, task-quality issue,
735	infrastructure failure, deterministic setup failure, or operator action
736	required. This classification feeds retry reporting and operator UX; it does
737	not replace the existing success/no-changes/failed outcome taxonomy.
738	
739	Both hooks are fail-open for infrastructure failures. If the skill package is
740	missing, the hook process crashes, evidence cannot be written, or the model
741	invocation fails, DDx records the hook error and continues with the underlying
742	attempt flow in WARN-ONLY mode. In BLOCK/factory mode, hook infrastructure
743	failure may skip the current candidate for the pass with observable evidence,
744	but it must not park the bead behind an unexplained cooldown. Only a
745	successfully computed readiness/lint result can block dispatch as an authoring
746	or scope failure. `--force --reason <text>` records an event with the actor,
747	reason, hook mode, and overridden criteria before proceeding.
748	
749	Long-running entrypoints perform a lightweight DDx runtime preflight before
750	starting work. This is not a full `ddx doctor` run: it avoids network checks,
751	package-manager checks, route probing, stale-document scans, and other broad
752	diagnostics. The preflight is limited to conditions that can predictably degrade
753	the command about to run:
754	
755	- project root resolution;
756	- `.ddx/config.yaml` parseability;
757	- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` parseability for bead-execution commands;
758	- the root `ddx` project skill under `.agents/skills/ddx/SKILL.md` or
759	  `.claude/skills/ddx/SKILL.md`;
760	- the nested `bead-lifecycle` skill when readiness/lint/triage hooks are wired;
761	- legacy project-local skill symlinks from the pre-FEAT-015 install model.
762	
763	Passing preflight checks are silent. Failed checks are observable and
764	actionable. `ddx work`, `ddx try`, and compatibility `ddx agent execute-loop`
765	fail on missing required project/tracker state, but warn once per process and
766	continue for optional lifecycle-hook degradation in WARN-ONLY mode. `ddx server`
767	fails on missing required server project state, but missing lifecycle skills or
768	legacy skill layout are surfaced as degraded startup diagnostics and health
769	metadata rather than blocking the HTTP server. When the lifecycle skill is
770	missing, both startup preflight and hook-time errors must include the project
771	root, the exact checked paths, and remediation:
772	
773	```text
774	Run:
775	  ddx update --force
776	  ddx doctor
777	```
778	
779	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains an `OutcomeReason` field beside the existing
780	`Disrupted` flag. `Disrupted` remains the coarse boolean that the attempt did
781	not complete normally. `OutcomeReason` is the machine-readable reason selected
782	by lifecycle classification, for example:
783	
784	- `bead_lint_warn`
785	- `bead_lint_blocked`
786	- `readiness_actionable_atomic`
787	- `readiness_rewritten`
788	- `readiness_decomposed`
789	- `readiness_needs_human`
790	- `forced_with_reason`
791	- `triage_prompt_quality`
792	- `triage_task_quality`
793	- `triage_infrastructure_failure`
794	- `triage_operator_action`
795	- `hook_unavailable_fail_open`
796	
797	Layer-3 `ddx work` records include the hook summaries for each child layer-2
798	attempt in the stop-condition evaluation log. A blocked lint result counts as a
799	terminal non-success outcome for that bead under the current policy, so the
800	existing `blocked`, `deferred`, and `no_progress` loop rules can reason about
801	it without a fourth run layer or a separate run type.
802	
803	## No Run-Type Catalog
804	
805	DDx will not introduce additional run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`.
806	Comparison, replay, benchmark, adversarial review, effort estimate,
807	bead breakdown, and similar workflows are **skill compositions** over
808	the task execution layers — they emit ordinary layer-1, layer-2, or layer-3
809	records, optionally tagged with a free-form `skill` label, and do not get
810	bespoke storage shapes, bespoke verbs, or bespoke spec sections.
811	
812	This is a load-bearing non-goal: every future "we should add a fourth
813	layer for X" proposal is rejected by reference to this section. If a
814	new workflow cannot be expressed as a composition over `run` / `try` /
815	`work`, the gap is in the task execution contract, not in the catalog.
816	
817	## Requirements
818	
819	### Functional
820	
821	1. **One on-disk substrate** — all run records, regardless of layer,
822	   persist under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with the common record shape and
823	   a discriminating `layer` field.
824	2. **Layer metadata** — every run record carries `layer`,
825	   `parent_run_id`, and the layer-appropriate extension subobject.
826	3. **Layer-1 invocation** — `ddx run` produces exactly one layer-1
827	   record per invocation; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract
828	   per CONTRACT-003.
829	4. **Layer-2 attempt** — `ddx try <bead>` produces exactly one layer-2
830	   record, references its child layer-1 records, and finalizes the
831	   worktree as `merge` or `preserve`.
832	5. **Layer-3 drain** — `ddx work` produces exactly one layer-3 record
833	   per invocation, references its child layer-2 attempt ids, and reports
834	   one of the terminal dispositions (`drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`,
835	   `no_progress`, `signal`, `resource_exhausted`).
836	6. **Stop conditions** — `ddx work` evaluates the stop conditions
837	   between attempts and persists the evaluation log on the layer-3
838	   record.
839	7. **No-progress detection** — the `no_progress` condition is mechanical
840	   (no commit, no merged side-effect) and configurable via
841	   `.ddx/config.yaml` (default `N = 3`).
842	8. **Append-only history** — a new run never rewrites a previous record
843	   at any layer.
844	9. **Cross-layer query** — `ddx runs` lists records across all three
845	   layers with layer filtering; `ddx tries` and `ddx work workers`
846	   are layer-specific projections over the same substrate.
847	10. **Run detail inspection** — for any run id, callers can retrieve the
848	    common fields, the layer extension, attachments, and child run ids.
849	11. **Generated-artifact provenance** — runs that regenerate an artifact
850	    set `produces_artifact: <id>` on the run record; FEAT-007 reads this
851	    to populate `generated_by` graph edges.
852	12. **Migration from legacy paths** — readers tolerate
853	    `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` during a
854	    documented migration window and surface them as layer-1 / layer-2
855	    records; writers always target `.ddx/runs/`.
856	13. **Configuration** — substrate root, no-progress threshold, deferred
857	    budget, and retention settings are configurable in
858	    `.ddx/config.yaml`.
859	14. **Execution cleanup** — `ddx try` and `ddx work` remove stale DDx-owned
860	    execution worktrees, DDx-created test/e2e scratch roots, generated test
861	    binaries, and liveness files through inline, loop, and background cleanup
862	    paths without deleting preserved attempts or published evidence.
863	15. **Resource preflight** — `ddx try` validates writable execution roots and
864	    free bytes/inodes before claim; failed validation may trigger one cleanup
865	    retry, and `ddx work` / server-managed workers run cleanup before claim
866	    whenever temp free space drops below a soft high-water threshold. If the
867	    temp roots remain below the hard floor after cleanup, the loop returns
868	    `resource_exhausted` without claiming.
869	
870	Expected implementation tests include `TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleDDXScratchDirs`,
871	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_RunsCleanupBelowSoftFloor`, and
872	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_StopsBelowHardFloorAfterCleanup`.
873	
874	### Non-Functional
875	
876	- **Determinism:** the persisted record reflects the exact prompt,
877	  response, logs, and structured result of one invocation.
878	- **Durability:** record writes are atomic or serialized so concurrent
879	  writers cannot leave partial records.
880	- **Observability:** stored records are human-readable enough for
881	  debugging and machine-parseable enough for automation.
882	- **Portability:** records remain repo-local and file-backed; no hosted
883	  service or database is required.
884	- **Low overhead:** layer-1 record bookkeeping adds minimal overhead
885	  beyond the underlying agent invocation.
886	- **Payload resilience:** large prompt, response, log, and bundle
887	  bodies are stored as attachments and never require rewriting a shared
888	  history file to persist one run safely.
889

922	
923	| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description | Mode |
924	|----------|--------------|-------------|------|
925	| `ddx_runs_list` | `GET /api/runs` | List run records across layers, filterable by `layer`, `artifact`, `bead` | read |
926	| `ddx_runs_show` | `GET /api/runs/:id` | Show one run record with layer extension and attachment refs | read |
927	| `ddx_runs_log` | `GET /api/runs/:id/log` | Raw logs / attachment bodies | read |
928	| `ddx_runs_result` | `GET /api/runs/:id/result` | Structured result payload | read |
929	| `ddx_artifact_regenerate` | `POST /api/artifacts/:id/regenerate` | Trigger regeneration of one artifact; returns the new run id | **write — narrow** |
930	| (GraphQL) | `Mutation.runRequeue` | Re-queue the originating bead of an existing run record (manual operator re-queue from the Runs UI); requires `idempotencyKey`; emits a canonical `run_requeue` audit event on the bead (schema below) | **write — narrow** |
931	
932	The HTTP/MCP read surface is total over the unified run substrate.
933	
934	The write surfaces added by this feature are limited to
935	`artifactRegenerate` and `runRequeue`. All other run invocation
936	(layer-1 ad-hoc, layer-2 bead attempts, layer-3 queue drain) remains
937	CLI-only. Additional write endpoints require a separate feature update;
938	they are not implicit in this read-coverage expansion.
939	
940	### Re-queue audit event schema
941	
942	The `runRequeue` mutation reopens the originating bead and appends a
943	`run_requeue` event to that bead's audit log. Concurrent or repeat
944	submissions with the same `idempotencyKey` collapse to a single
945	re-queue and a single event (`deduplicated=true` is returned to all
946	subsequent callers); if the cached idempotency record points at a
947	missing bead, the requeue is replayed against the run's current
948	originating bead. The event uses the standard `bead.BeadEvent`
949	envelope and the following field schema:
950	
951	| Field | Value |
952	|---|---|
953	| `kind` | `run_requeue` (`RunRequeueEventKind`) |
954	| `summary` | `run requeued` |
955	| `actor` | Resolved operator identity; falls back to `anonymous` when no request identity is available |
956	| `source` | `graphql:runRequeue` |
957	| `body` | Single line `key=value` payload: `identity=<identity.kind> actor=<identity.actor> run_id=<runId> idempotency_key=<key> layer_override=<layer-or-empty>` |
958	
959	The body line is structured for grep/jq parsing: each token is a
960	`key=value` pair separated by single spaces. `layer_override` is the
961	empty string when the caller did not pass `RunRequeueInput.layer`.
962	`identity.kind` is the resolver-selected operator identity class
963	(`unknown`, `localhost`, or `tsnet`). `actor` is the resolved user or
964	operator label; when the request carries no identifying header, the
965	audit fields fall back to `identity=unknown actor=anonymous`.
966	
967	The GraphQL mutation response may also report `deduplicated=true`, but
968	that is a write-response flag, not part of the persisted audit event.
969	
970	Consumers of the audit log (FEAT-008 Runs row expansion, evaluation
971	skills under FEAT-019) treat `run_requeue` events as the canonical
972	record that a re-queue happened: there is no separate persisted
973	`requeue` record beyond (a) the new `open` status on the originating
974	bead and (b) this event entry.
975	
976	### Run-detail access audit events
977	
978	The first time the canonical project-scoped run detail is resolved for a
979	run record that is visible through project membership, DDx appends a
980	`run_detail_view` event to the originating bead's audit log. This is the
981	canonical record that a human inspected the run-detail surface.
982	
983	The event is read-side only: it is emitted on the canonical detail load,
984	not on in-page tab switches, evidence downloads, or breadcrumb/back
985	navigation. If the caller cannot see the project, there is no run detail
986	and therefore no audit event.
987	
988	The `body` line is structured for grep/jq parsing and includes the
989	project-scoped identity of the inspection:
990	
991	| Field | Value |
992	|---|---|
993	| `kind` | `run_detail_view` |
994	| `summary` | `run detail viewed` |
995	| `actor` | Viewer identity derived from the authenticated project member |
996	| `source` | `graphql:run` |
997	| `body` | Single line: `project_id=<projectId> run_id=<runId> layer=<layer> visibility=project_membership` |
998	
999	### Layer-to-substrate mapping for the Runs UI
1000	
1001	The web Runs view (FEAT-008 §5, FEAT-021) renders three layer chips
1002	backed by the unified substrate plus the legacy detail backings. The
1003	shared run-detail tab strip is the canonical drill-down surface used by
1004	FEAT-008, FEAT-019, and FEAT-021:
1005	
1006	| Chip | Substrate row | Detail backing for row expansion |
1007	|---|---|---|
1008	| `work` | `layer: 3` Run record | Layer-3 record's queue inputs / stop-condition log / child layer-2 attempt ids; the shared tab strip exposes `overview` only |
1009	| `try` | `layer: 2` Run record | The `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle attached to the layer-2 record (manifest, prompt, result, checks, verdict); the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
1010	| `run` | `layer: 1` Run record | The associated `AgentSession` row (prompt / response / stderr / billing / cached-token detail) joined onto the layer-1 record; the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
1011	
1012	`AgentSession` rows that have no parent layer-2 attempt (raw `ddx
1013	agent log` invocations) surface as synthesized `layer=run` Runs rows
1014	keyed by session id, so no agent-session row is dropped during the
1015	Sessions/Executions tab retirement.
1016	
1017	The shared tab strip is:
1018	
1019	- `work` → `overview`
1020	- `try` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, `evidence`
1021	- `run` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, `evidence`
1022	
1023	## User Stories
1024	
1025	### US-090: Developer Inspects a Layer-1 Run
1026	
1027	**As a** developer running an ad-hoc agent invocation
1028	**I want** to inspect the resulting run record
1029	**So that** I can audit prompt, response, tokens, and side-effects
1030	
1031	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1032	- Given I invoke `ddx run`, then DDx writes one layer-1 record under
1033	  `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1` and the layer-1 extension
1034	  populated.
1035	- Given the run completes, when I run `ddx runs show <run-id>`, then I
1036	  see common fields, the layer-1 extension, requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`,
1037	  actual model/power, requested passthrough constraints if supplied, and attachment
1038	  references.
1039	
1040	### US-091: Developer Inspects a Layer-2 Bead Attempt
1041	
1042	**As a** developer reviewing a bead attempt
1043	**I want** to see the attempt's worktree finalization and the child
1044	layer-1 invocations
1045	**So that** I can audit what the agent did inside the attempt
1046	
1047	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1048	- Given I invoke `ddx try <bead-id>`, then DDx writes one layer-2
1049	  record with `layer: 2` referencing one or more layer-1 run ids.
1050	- Given the attempt finalizes, when I run `ddx runs show <run-id>`,
1051	  then the record reports `merge` or `preserve` and surfaces the
1052	  evidence bundle.
1053	
1054	### US-092: Developer Drains the Queue and Audits the Drain
1055	
1056	**As a** developer running `ddx work`
1057	**I want** the loop to stop on a clearly named condition
1058	**So that** I can tell whether the queue was drained, blocked, deferred,
1059	spinning, or interrupted
1060	
1061	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1062	- Given I invoke `ddx work`, then DDx writes exactly one layer-3 record
1063	  per invocation referencing each child layer-2 attempt id.
1064	- Given I press Ctrl-C while `ddx work` or `ddx try` is running, then DDx
1065	  prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully` before cleanup begins.
1066	- Given the loop stops, then the layer-3 record's terminal disposition
1067	  is one of `drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `no_progress`, `signal`,
1068	  and the evaluation log names which condition fired and on which
1069	  iteration.
1070	
1071	### US-093: No-Progress Detection Stops a Spinning Loop
1072	
1073	**As a** developer running `ddx work`
1074	**I want** the loop to stop when consecutive attempts make no progress
1075	**So that** I do not burn budget on a pathological queue state
1076	
1077	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1078	- Given `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produce no commit and no
1079	  merged side-effect (default `N = 3`, configurable), then the next
1080	  stop-condition evaluation triggers `no_progress` and the layer-3 record
1081	  terminates with that disposition.
1082	- Given a candidate is skipped by readiness, claim race, routing preflight,
1083	  quota/transport/auth/tool setup failure, review error, or operator-action
1084	  class, then that iteration does not increment the no-progress counter.
1085	- Given retry policy permits escalation before `no_progress` fires, when
1086	  a retry is scheduled, then the next layer-1 run raises `MinPower`
1087	  and records the actual model/power returned by the agent.
1088	- Given the original invocation supplied `--harness`, `--provider`, or
1089	  `--model`, then retries pass the same values through unchanged and DDx does
1090	  not inspect those values when choosing the next requested `MinPower`.
1091	- Given retry escalation would exceed the power available under hard
1092	  passthrough pins, then DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
1093	  `agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the Fizeau-supplied evidence, and does not
1094	  mutate the pins or call `ResolveRoute`.
1095	
1096	### US-094: Cross-Layer Run History
1097	
1098	**As a** workflow tool
1099	**I want** to query run history across the task execution layers from one place
1100	**So that** I do not have to merge two on-disk shapes by hand
1101	
1102	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1103	- Given `run`, `try`, and `work` records exist, when a tool calls
1104	  `GET /api/runs?layer=...`, then it receives records of the requested
1105	  layer with consistent common fields.
1106	- Given a layer-3 record exists, then its child layer-2 attempt ids and
1107	  their child layer-1 run ids are reachable by following
1108	  `parent_run_id` pointers.
1109	
1110	### US-095: Artifact Regeneration Records Provenance
1111	
1112	**As a** consumer of the document graph
1113	**I want** generated artifacts to point back to the run that produced
1114	them
1115	**So that** staleness can be evaluated against generator inputs
1116	
1117	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1118	- Given I regenerate an artifact via `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`,
1119	  then the resulting run record has `produces_artifact: <id>`.
1120	- Given a generated artifact has been produced by a run, then
1121	  FEAT-007's `generated_by` edge resolves to that run id.
1122	
1123	### US-096: Operator Migrates From Legacy Run Trees
1124	
1125	**As a** repo operator adopting the unified substrate
1126	**I want** legacy `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`
1127	records to remain inspectable during the migration window
1128	**So that** adopting `.ddx/runs/` does not strand prior evidence
1129	
1130	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1131	- Given a repository contains legacy run data, when DDx reads run
1132	  history, then legacy paths surface through the unified read API as
1133	  layer-1 / layer-2 records during the documented migration window.
1134	- Given the migration window closes, then writers target only
1135	  `.ddx/runs/` and a one-shot rewrite has normalized prior records into
1136	  the unified substrate.
1137	
1138	## CONTRACT-003 Amendment
1139	
1140	This feature requires two narrow read-only-amendment-adjacent changes
1141	to CONTRACT-003: write surfaces for `artifactRegenerate` (HTTP/MCP) and
1142	`runRequeue` (GraphQL). `runRequeue` does not invoke the execution service —
1143	it reopens the originating bead so the existing `ddx work` loop can claim it
1144	again — but it is still a write because it mutates bead state and appends an
1145	audit event (see "Re-queue audit event schema" above).
1146	No other write surfaces are added; in particular, layer-1, layer-2,
1147	and layer-3 invocation remain CLI-only.
1148	
1149	If FEAT-006 implementation reveals additional CONTRACT-003 gaps (e.g.,
1150	upstream Cobra root export details), those amendments are scoped to
1151	FEAT-006 and not to this feature.
1152	
1153	## Dependencies
1154	
1155	- FEAT-005 (Artifacts) — `produces_artifact` references artifact IDs
1156	- FEAT-006 (Fizeau consumer contract) — provides the layer-1 consumer-side wrapper
1157	  and CONTRACT-003 boundary
1158	- FEAT-007 (Doc Graph) — `generated_by` edges read `produces_artifact`
1159	  from run records
1160	- DDx CLI infrastructure (config loading, command factory)
1161	
1162	## Out of Scope
1163	
1164	- **Run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`** — comparison, replay, benchmark,
1165	  adversarial review, effort estimate, and similar workflows are skill
1166	  compositions, not new layers or new on-disk shapes.
1167	- **Content-aware supervisory loop decisions** — `ddx work` performs
1168	  mechanical queue drain only. Decisions like "comparison failed →
1169	  enqueue reconciliation beads" are skill / plugin / workflow-tool
1170	  territory.
1171	- **Server-side layer-1/2/3 invocation** — only `artifactRegenerate`
1172	  has a write endpoint in this feature.
1173	- **Workflow-specific action semantics** — phase routing, methodology
1174	  scoring, tool-specific issue closing — delegated to workflow tools.
1175	- **Workflow-specific autonomy semantics** — DDx owns the mechanical retry,
1176	  review, cooldown, and budget policy in this feature and ADR-024. Higher-level
1177	  methodology decisions such as "file reconciliation beads after a benchmark"
1178	  remain workflow-tool territory.
1179	- **Hosted run history storage** — records are repo-local and
1180	  file-backed.
1181	- **Separate domain-specific runtime stores** — there is one substrate.

9	# ADR-023: Bead Lifecycle Quality Policy
10	
11	**Status:** Proposed (Accepted after operator review)
12	**Date:** 2026-05-04
13	**Authors:** bead `ddx-9210f95a`
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	Reliability principle P7 ("BEAD = PROMPT") is planned in
18	`docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md` by bead `ddx-06b77652`.
19	The principle says a bead's description and acceptance criteria must be enough
20	for a competent sub-agent to execute without hand-curation, but it does not
21	define the enforcement mechanism, waiver model, staged rollout, or recovery UX.
22	
23	The quality gap is already visible in evidence:
24	
25	- Audit 1, `.ddx/executions/20260503T195715-4725673a/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`,
26	  found repeated misses in root-cause detail, concrete test names, and
27	  self-contained scope.
28	- Audit 1 snapshot,
29	  `.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`,
30	  directly operationalizes P7 and records the first 20-bead scoring pass.
31	- Audit 2,
32	  `.ddx/executions/20260504T030000-audit2/bead-quality-audit-remaining-2026-05-04.md`,
33	  extended the scan across the remaining open queue and calls out evidence bead
34	  `ddx-f339c399`.
35	- `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` is the canonical
36	  8-criterion authoring rubric and explicitly defines valid criterion skips for
37	  epic, doc-only, deletion, and rename beads.
38	
39	Three existing feature specs already own the affected surfaces:
40	
41	- FEAT-004 owns bead schema, validation hooks, labels, and evidence.
42	- FEAT-010 owns `ddx try`, `ddx work`, run records, and attempt outcomes.
43	- FEAT-011 owns the DDx skill packaging model that gives agents reusable
44	  workflow guidance.
45	
46	## Decision
47	
48	DDx adopts bead-lifecycle quality enforcement as policy over the existing
49	bead, execution, and skill surfaces. No new bead schema fields are introduced.
50	No new top-level FEAT is created.
51	
52	### Policy
53	
54	Every automated bead attempt has two quality checkpoints:
55	
56	1. **Bead readiness assessment.** Before `ddx work` claims a bead or `ddx try`
57	   launches the implementation invocation, DDx evaluates whether the bead is
58	   tractable and actionable. It checks the bead description, acceptance
59	   criteria, labels, parent, dependency metadata, spec-id, prior attempt
60	   history, and the canonical rubric in
61	   `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`. The implementation hook
62	   may still advertise `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility only; the
63	   canonical product concept is bead readiness assessment.
64	2. **Post-attempt triage.** After an attempt finalizes, DDx triages the result
65	   against the same lifecycle quality policy so a low-quality prompt failure,
66	   missing rationale, empty review block, or structurally ambiguous outcome is
67	   classified and surfaced consistently.
68	
69	The readiness checkpoint and the post-attempt triage checkpoint are distinct:
70	readiness decides whether a bead should be claimed or rewritten before
71	execution, lint/rubric scoring measures prompt quality inside the readiness
72	evaluation, and triage classifies the attempt after evidence exists.
73	
74	Both checkpoints invoke the same nested bead-lifecycle workflow skill under the
75	`ddx` skill tree. The skill is responsible for translating the rubric into
76	agent-readable findings; DDx remains responsible for hook timing, evidence
77	placement, and outcome classification.
78	
79	Post-attempt triage classifications feed
80	[`TD-031`](../technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md). ADR-023 does not
81	define final queue mutation policy; TD-031 remains the source of truth for
82	whether an attempt closes, stays open for human triage, becomes blocked, is
83	superseded, or receives a retry cooldown.
84	
85	### Staged Rollout And Factory Mode
86	
87	The default rollout is WARN-ONLY.
88	
89	- WARN-ONLY mode reports the lint score, missing criteria, waiver matches, and
90	  suggested remediation, then proceeds with dispatch.
91	- BLOCK mode is opt-in until the queue baseline confirms that ordinary
92	  execution-ready beads can consistently satisfy the rubric.
93	- BLOCK mode may stop only on a valid low lint score after applicable rubric
94	  skips and label waivers are applied. It must not stop on hook crashes,
95	  missing skill files, transient filesystem errors, or malformed lint output.
96	- Reliable factory mode is BLOCK mode plus default adversarial review. In that
97	  mode, poorly specified work is improved, decomposed, or blocked before claim
98	  rather than allowed to consume implementation attempts.
99	
100	This makes the policy measurable before it becomes a dispatch gate and avoids
101	freezing the queue while legacy beads are being retrofitted.
102	
103	### Waiver Model
104	
105	Waivers exist at two levels:
106	
107	- **Rubric-level by bead type.** The skips documented in
108	  `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` are built into lint:
109	  doc-only beads may omit criterion (c) and (d), epic beads may satisfy those
110	  criteria through children, and pure deletion or rename beads may omit
111	  criterion (d) while preserving behavior.
112	- **Per-bead labels.** Rare exceptions use labels of the form
113	  `lint-waiver:<criterion>`, for example `lint-waiver:c`. Labels are the
114	  durable waiver store because FEAT-004 already defines labels as free-form
115	  bead metadata and no schema change is warranted.
116	
117	Operator override uses `--force --reason <text>`. The override records an event
118	in the bead evidence stream naming the criterion, mode, actor, reason, and lint
119	summary. It does not silently bypass the hook and it does not mutate the rubric.
120	
121	### Fail-Open Behavior
122	
123	The lifecycle hooks follow reliability principle P1's fail-open posture for
124	infrastructure failures.
125	
126	- If the hook cannot run, the workflow skill is missing, output cannot be
127	  parsed, or evidence cannot be written, DDx records the infrastructure failure
128	  and proceeds.
129	- In WARN-ONLY mode, all valid lint results proceed after reporting.
130	- In BLOCK mode, only a valid post-waiver lint result below the configured
131	  threshold blocks dispatch.
132	- Post-attempt triage failures never erase the attempt result. They annotate the
133	  report and evidence so retry policy and operators can distinguish "agent made
134	  a bad attempt" from "quality infrastructure failed."
135	
136	### Safe Improvement, Decomposition, And Recovery UX
137	
138	Bead readiness assessment may update a bead before execution only when the
139	update is intent-preserving and grounded in durable context. Safe improvements
140	include validated replacement rewrites of the description, normalizing the bead
141	body into the authoring template, adding discovered file:line evidence, adding
142	an obvious subsystem test command, and wiring deterministic labels, parent, or
143	dependencies.
144	
145	Validated replacement is preferred over append-only amendment when replacement
146	would make the bead a better implementation prompt. Prompt fitness is part of
147	the quality policy: readiness should leave the next agent enough durable context
148	to execute without dragging along stale, duplicated, or chat-shaped noise. A
149	one-line vague bead may need to expand into a standalone task; a noisy bead may
150	need to shrink. The original bead text remains auditable through the readiness
151	event and before/after hashes; it does not have to be embedded verbatim in the
152	replacement prompt body.
153	
154	The validator proves preservation from durable anchors rather than asking
155	whether the new bead is merely "in the spirit" of the old one. Acceptance
156	criteria, non-scope, governing artifact references, dependencies, named
157	files/tests, and still-valid root-cause evidence are explicit commitments and
158	must be preserved or replaced with current durable evidence. If the old bead
159	contains contradictions, stale anchors, or missing product choices, readiness may
160	remove the confusing wording only when the replacement clearly marks the
161	unresolved part as `needs_human` or preserves it as an explicit constraint.
162	Readiness must block for human input instead of inventing acceptance criteria,
163	changing scope, choosing between conflicting requirements, deleting unresolved
164	constraints, or guessing a missing governing artifact.
165	
166	If readiness finds the bead too broad, it decomposes before claim. Every parent AC
167	must map to at least one child AC or be explicitly marked `needs_human` or
168	`non_scope`; token-overlap metrics are heuristics, not proof of preservation.
169	The parent is blocked/decomposed with child ids and the AC map in evidence.
170	
171	When BLOCK mode stops dispatch, the operator-facing output must be actionable.
172	It prints:
173	
174	- bead id, score, mode, and blocked criteria
175	- the missing fields or malformed sections in plain language
176	- the waiver labels already applied and the labels that would waive each
177	  failed criterion
178	- suggested `ddx bead update` commands for adding description, acceptance,
179	  labels, parent, notes, or dependency metadata
180	- the `--force --reason` form for exceptional dispatch, with the note that an
181	  evidence event will be recorded
182	
183	Recovery output must not require reading the lint implementation or an
184	out-of-band report to fix an ordinary authoring problem.
185	
186	## Consequences
187	
188	- FEAT-004 owns the label-based waiver storage and the no-new-schema rule.
189	- FEAT-010 owns hook insertion in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, attempt
190	  classification, and the `OutcomeReason` report field.
191	- FEAT-011 owns nested workflow-skill packaging so the same rubric guidance can
192	  be reused by lint, triage, review, breakdown, replay, and benchmark flows.
193	- Legacy beads can continue running in WARN-ONLY while the queue is baselined.
194	- Once BLOCK mode is enabled, dispatch quality becomes explicit and auditable
195	  instead of depending on operator memory.
196	
197	## Non-Goals
198	
199	- A new FEAT for bead quality; the existing feature specs own the affected
200	  surfaces.
201	- The separate `in_progress` eligibility bug.
202	- Cross-project skill packaging, which remains deferred to FEAT-015.
203	- New fields in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`; waivers use labels and evidence events.
204	
205	## References
206

208	- `ddx-f339c399` — evidence bead called out by Audit 2.

210	  criterion-skip policy.

212	  — Audit 1.
